calendar.matters:
Notice Of Hearing On Petition For Writ Of Administrative Mandate
Off Calendar Jan-30-2017 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Mitchell J. Green, of Nossaman LLP, Tel. 415.398.3600, for petitioners;
Jennifer A. Bunshoft, of the Office of the Attorney General, Tel. 415.703.5085, for respondents.

Ruling: A R G U E D. The Court adopts its tentative ruling. PETITIONER DEAN FREEDLANDER Petition For Writ Of Administrative Mandate is denied. Dr. Freedlander is not entitled to writ relief by administrative mandate per CCP 1094.5 because Respondent California Department of Health Care Services was not required by law to hold an evidentiary hearing. (See Scott v. Board of Trustees (2013) 217 Cal.App.4th 117, 122-23; 22 CCR 51459.) When a provider has been suspended from participation in the Medi-Cal program, the provider may petition for reinstatement. (22 CCR 51459.) The applicable regulation does not require the Department hold an evidentiary hearing but rather states, "the petitioner shall be afforded an opportunity to present written argument to the Department. The Department shall decide the petition, and the decision shall include the reasons therefor." (Ibid.) There is no legally cognizable interest at issue that would require the court to use an independent judgment standard, as the only interest in dispute is Dr. Freedlaner's continued participation in the Medi-Cal program. California law mirrors federal law in that "[p]roviders of Medicare services or other related programs have no protected interest in continued participation in such programs." (Lin v. State of Cal. (2000) 78 Cal.App.4th 931, 935; see Guzman v. Shewry (9th Cir. 2008) 552 F.3d 941, 954.) Because there is no protected interest at issue and therefore no requirement that the court undertake an independent review, the court must uphold the Department's decision unless it finds the determination was "arbitrary, capricious, or entirely lacking evidentiary support." (State Bd. of Chiropractic Examiners v. Sup. Ct. (2009) 45 Cal.4th 963, 977.) The court finds that the Department's determination not to reinstate Dr. Freedlander in the Medi-Cal program is not arbitrary, capricious, or entirely lacking evidentiary support. Dr. Freedlander does not dispute his January 2003 criminal conviction or that allegations of inappropriate sexual acts were made against him. Despite the Department's statement that there was no evidence of rehabilitation, the Department stated it "review[ed] all the information available" and concluded that it "cannot be reasonably certain that these sexual acts have not recurred and will not recur if presented with the opportunity." (Granda Declaration, Exh. I.) The Department primarily relied upon Dr. Freedlander's prior conviction and evidence showing the nature of allegations made against him. (Ibid.) The Department used the appropriate standard to grant reinstatement "only if it finds it is reasonably certain that the types of actions that formed the basis for the original exclusion have not recurred and will not recur." (42 CFR 1002.215(a).) The reasons articulated in the Department's May 18, 2016 denial letter suffice to show its decision was in no way arbitrary, capricious, or entirely lacking in evidentiary support. Prevailing party shall prepare form of order that complies with CRC 3.1312. Judge: A. James Robertson II; Clerk: Felicia Green; Court Reporter: Gena Eales, CSR No. 9176, Email: geales@sftc.org; Dept. 302.
